items.q: How do stakeholder and shareholder interests overlap and conflict?
items.correct: Shareholders are a subset of stakeholders. Their interests overlap (long-term success benefits all) but conflict on issues like dividends vs investment, wages vs profits, or short-term gains vs long-term sustainability.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders always want the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Shareholders are external and stakeholders are internal.
items.why: The shareholder-stakeholder distinction is central to debates on corporate purpose. Students miss the structural tension — a board can't always please everyone simultaneously, which is what stakeholder analysis exists to examine.

items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: Which is a stakeholder but not a shareholder?
items.why: Correct answer: Local community near the factory. “Owner of preference shares” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Mendelow's matrix uses which two axes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Power and Interest. “Profit and Risk” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many bottom lines in the triple bottom line?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
